start.subtitle: No chatbot, no popup. Just a few honest questions.

options.label: I'd rather read on my own

neue-art.subtitle: Good. New website – there are different directions.

neue-umfang.subtitle: 14+ industries in 26 years. Something fitting is there.

neue-cms.text: Do you manage content yourself or does someone do it for you?
neue-cms.subtitle: Important question. The right technology depends on it.

options.label: We manage it ourselves (text, images, news)

info-cms-vergleich.text: WordPress makes sense if your team regularly updates text, images or news. You log in, change something, done. Static HTML is faster, more secure and cheaper to run – but changes require a developer.
info-cms-vergleich.detail: Rule of thumb: More than 2 changes per month? WordPress. Fewer? Static is enough.

info-shop-einfach.text: Under 50 products: check if Shopify is enough. Ready-made themes, quick to launch, low maintenance. Costs ~30 EUR/month plus 2% per transaction. WooCommerce is yours (no subscription) but needs hosting and more setup.
info-shop-einfach.detail: WooCommerce pays off when you need custom processes – special pricing, integrations, individual checkout flows.

portal-was.subtitle: Portals and tools – my favourite territory.

problem-art.subtitle: OK. What exactly is the problem?

redesign-was.subtitle: Not everything that looks old needs a relaunch.

performance-cms.subtitle: The cause often depends on the CMS.

bfsg-status.subtitle: 96% of all websites still have accessibility issues.

options.label: Yes, but the errors are unclear
Yes, I need help fixing them

info-bfsg-quickcheck.text: Three quick tests you can run right now: 1. Keyboard test: Can you navigate your website with just Tab and Enter, no mouse? 2. Contrast test: Is light text on a light background readable? Install the WAVE browser extension. 3. Image test: Do all images have alt texts? Right-click, Inspect, search for alt=.
info-bfsg-quickcheck.detail: If any of the three tests fails, you have accessibility action items. The good news: most errors can be fixed quickly.

options.label: I still want a professional check

seo-wo.subtitle: Google and AI systems are two different problems.

technik-cms.subtitle: So I can help more specifically.

betreuung-umfang.subtitle: Maintenance. I do this for 20+ clients.

options.label: Full service: one contact for everything

geo-wissen.text: How much do you know about AI visibility?
geo-wissen.subtitle: Good topic. Few are paying attention to this so far.

options.label: Basic idea clear – how do you do it?
I want an audit of my website

geo-info.text: Short version: 73% of B2B buyers research with ChatGPT. 61% fewer clicks on classic Google results since AI Overviews. If AI systems can't understand your website, you don't exist for these users.
geo-info.detail: I structure websites so that AI systems can correctly capture and cite them. I do this in every project. Not because it's free, but because it's pointless to leave it out.

kosten-art.text: What do you need a price estimate for?
kosten-art.subtitle: Honest numbers. No starting-at prices that triple.

info-kosten-website.text: Realistic 2026 prices for company websites: 5-8 pages, responsive, accessible: EUR 3,000 to 6,000. Medium with sections, multilingual: EUR 6,000 to 15,000. Corporate group, complex: EUR 15,000 to 40,000. Plus hosting (EUR 5-30/month) and optional maintenance.
info-kosten-website.detail: My hourly rate: EUR 80 net. I bill by effort, not gut feeling. Before the project starts, you get an estimate I stick to.

info-kosten-guenstig.text: Cheaper options exist. Static HTML instead of WordPress saves hosting and maintenance. Fewer pages means less effort. And if you supply text and images yourself instead of me developing content, the price drops significantly.
info-kosten-guenstig.detail: What I don't recommend: site builders like Wix or Jimdo. They cost more long-term because you never truly own it and will eventually need to migrate anyway. And they can't do accessibility properly.

options.label: Got it – I want a website

info-kosten-shop.text: Functional WooCommerce shop: from EUR 5,000. With custom processes (B2B, integrations): EUR 10,000 to 25,000. Plus: hosting (EUR 10-50/month), payment fees where applicable.
info-kosten-shop.detail: Shopify is cheaper to set up (ready-made themes) but more expensive to run: EUR 29+/month plus 2% transaction fee. From ~300 orders/month, WooCommerce becomes more economical.

options.label: I want to build a shop

info-kosten-betreuung.text: Basic (updates, backups, monitoring): from EUR 15/month. Standard (plus changes, content): from EUR 80/month. Premium (dedicated developer, priority): EUR 400-800/month.
info-kosten-betreuung.detail: All plans cancellable monthly. No 12-month contract. Most small websites only need Basic.

info-kosten-stunde.text: My hourly rate: EUR 80 net. Small tasks (change text, swap image): 15-30 minutes. Debug a plugin issue: 1-3 hours. Build a new subpage: 3-8 hours.
info-kosten-stunde.detail: I estimate the effort upfront and stick to it. If it takes longer than estimated, that's on me.

vergleich-was.subtitle: Good that you're comparing. Everyone should.

info-freelancer-vs-agentur.text: Agency: multiple specialists, project manager, processes. Good for large teams and high budgets. Downside: people rotate, you explain yourself every time. Freelancer: one person who knows your project from start to finish. Faster paths, lower overhead.
info-freelancer-vs-agentur.detail: The most common objection to freelancers: What if they get sick? My answer: I document everything so another developer could take over at any time. And I haven't been unavailable in 26 years.

info-freelancer-auswaehlen.text: Five questions for every freelancer before you sign: 1. Can I see a live website you built alone? 2. What happens to my website if you quit? 3. Is accessibility (BFSG/EAA) included in your offer? 4. How quickly do you respond to problems? 5. What does an hour of post-project work cost?
info-freelancer-auswaehlen.detail: Anyone who can answer all five clearly is probably fine. Anyone who dodges is not.

options.label: I want to hear your answers

info-selber-vs-profi.text: DIY works for: simple landing pages (Carrd, GitHub Pages), blogs (WordPress.com), small shops (Shopify with standard theme). You need a pro for: custom processes, accessibility, performance optimization, structured data, anything that should still be maintainable after three years.
info-selber-vs-profi.detail: Rule of thumb: If the website should make money or win clients, a pro pays off. If it just needs to exist, self-service is fine.

result-website.title: Three things good company websites in 2026 have in common.
result-website.text:
First: few pages, clear structure. Most companies need 5 to 8 pages, not 40. Second: the website must be readable for AI systems, not just for Google. 73% of B2B buyers research with ChatGPT. Third: accessibility per WCAG 2.1 AA has been mandatory since June 2025 (European Accessibility Act), not optional. Many don't know this yet.

Typical effort: 40–80 hours, depending on scope.

cta.label: Send me your ideas – I’ll reply within one business day

result-website-wp.title: WordPress – but done right.
result-website-wp.text:
WordPress is the right choice if your team wants to manage content themselves. But: not every theme and not every page builder is good. Elementor and Divi are popular but generate bloated code and slow your site down. I work with YOOtheme Pro or the native block editor – clean code, faster load times, no plugin lock-in. AI visibility and accessibility are built into every project. Copy edited by my wife Annemarie.

Typical effort: 50–100 hours incl. setup and training.

cta.label: Let’s talk about your WordPress project

result-website-statisch.title: Static HTML – less is more.
result-website-statisch.text:
If a developer manages the content, you don't need a CMS. Static websites load faster, are more secure (no login, no database, no updates) and cost almost nothing to run. GitHub Pages or Netlify: EUR 0 hosting. This website here is static. Lighthouse 100, no framework, no build tool. Push to the repo, live instantly. The downside: for every text change you need me or a developer. At less than 2 changes per month, that's not a problem.

Typical effort: 30–60 hours. Minimal running costs afterwards.

cta.label: Tell me about your project

result-website-gross.title: Multiple websites – the most common problem isn't the technology.
result-website-gross.text:
When several websites belong together, projects rarely fail because of WordPress or design. They fail because of coordination. Every department wants something different, nobody has the overview. The solution is simple: one person who knows all websites and has all contacts. No agency team where you have to explain who needs what every time. I've managed projects like this for years – multiple websites, one person, and it worked because everything went through me.

Effort depends on scope. Comparable projects: 100–300 hours.

cta.label: Let’s discuss the coordination

result-shop.title: Honest assessment: WooCommerce vs. Shopify.
result-shop.text:
Shopify is easier at first. Ready-made themes, quick to launch. But you're renting: monthly fees, transaction costs, limited customisation. WooCommerce is yours. You pay for hosting and development, but the shop belongs to you. For standard products with few special requirements, Shopify may suffice. Once you need custom processes – special pricing, integrations, custom checkout – WooCommerce becomes more economical. Most common e-commerce mistake: too complex too early. Start with what you actually need.

Typical effort: 80–150 hours, depending on process complexity.

cta.label: Tell me about your sales processes

result-shop-gross.title: Large catalogue – infrastructure matters.
result-shop-gross.text:
From 500 products, you need more than a good theme. Shared hosting dies at this scale. You need managed hosting (from EUR 30/month), a staging environment for testing and proper image compression (otherwise product pages become painful). Plus: solid import/export for product data, filters that stay fast at 1,000 products, and an SEO plan for large catalogues. This is doable but not a weekend project.

Typical effort: 100–200 hours incl. infrastructure and import.

cta.label: Send me the catalogue scope – I’ll estimate the effort

result-shop-b2b.title: B2B shops need different things than B2C.
result-shop-b2b.text:
In B2C, a person buys one product. In B2B, a buyer orders 500 units, needs tiered pricing, a dealer portal, maybe approval from their manager. No standard theme handles that. A B2B shop must be a tool for your sales team, not a pretty catalogue. Since 2023 I've been running an industrial client with exactly this setup: WooCommerce, dealer portal, sample tracking. Sole technical responsibility. It works because the shop was built around the sales processes, not the other way around.

Typical effort: 100–200 hours. Dealer portal and integrations take time.

cta.label: Tell me about your B2B sales process

result-portal-karriere.title: Career portal – buy off the shelf or build custom?
result-portal-karriere.text:
If you just want to post jobs: JOIN, Personio or Recruitee can do that. EUR 50-300/month, quick to launch, applicant management included. Custom makes sense when your portal needs more: your own design, integration into your website, onboarding processes, location filters, multilingual support. I built a career portal plus internal onboarding tool for the Runden Group. No standard tool would have covered it because the processes were too specific.

Typical effort: 60–120 hours, depending on integration depth.

cta.label: Describe your recruiting process to me

result-portal-kunden.title: Customer portal – what to watch out for.
result-portal-kunden.text:
Login area means: user management, passwords, sessions, data protection. That's not trivial. For WordPress there are solutions (MemberPress, WooCommerce Memberships), for static sites you need a backend. Important: GDPR. Personal data behind a login requires a processing register, a privacy policy that covers it, and encrypted transmission (HTTPS). Sounds involved, but it's routine when planned from the start.

Typical effort: 60–120 hours, GDPR concept included.

cta.label: Tell me what customers should see

result-portal-intern.title: Internal tool – do you really need custom?
result-portal-intern.text:
Honest question. Project management? Asana or Notion. CRM? HubSpot or Pipedrive. Time tracking? Clockify. Custom development only pays off when your process doesn't fit an existing tool. The line is where your data needs to flow between systems or where standard tools bend your workflow instead of supporting it. If 80% works with an off-the-shelf tool, use the tool. The last 20% can be solved with integrations.

Effort varies widely. Initial consultation clarifies the scope.

cta.label: Let’s talk about your project

result-portal-bericht.title: Interactive report – right up my alley.
result-portal-bericht.text:
Annual reports, impact reports, sustainability reports – as PDFs they gather dust. As interactive websites they get read, shared and linked. I built a digital impact report for Seniorpartner in School with an interactive SVG map of Germany and infographics. Static HTML, no backend, runs on any server. The beauty of reports: clear scope, clear end, measurable result.

Typical effort: 40–80 hours. Clear scope, clear end.

cta.label: Send me the current report – I’ll draft a concept

result-landingpage.title: What a landing page needs – and what it doesn't.
result-landingpage.text:
One goal. One CTA. No navigation menu that leads visitors away. The most common mistakes: too much text, too many options, no clear next step. A landing page is not a miniature website but a focused funnel. Static HTML almost always suffices. Faster than WordPress, no maintenance overhead, hosting costs almost nothing. If one page is enough, I won't sell you ten.

Typical effort: 15–30 hours. Static, quick to deliver.

cta.label: Send me the offer – I’ll design the page

result-redesign.title: Redesign or facelift? Here's how to find out.
result-redesign.text:
Three questions: Is the technology outdated (PHP 7, no HTTPS, not responsive)? Then redesign. Is the content wrong (outdated services, old staff, old photos)? Then a content update often suffices. Has your audience changed? Then you need a new concept, not just a new design. Many agencies sell a relaunch when an update would be enough. I look at what's there and honestly tell you what's worth it.

Typical effort: 30–60 hours, depending on how much changes.

cta.label: Send me the URL – I’ll tell you what’s worth it

result-redesign-technik.title: Outdated technology – the foundation must be right.
result-redesign-technik.text: If the site isn't responsive or runs on PHP 7, no new design will help. First fix the base: current PHP, HTTPS, responsive layout, performance. Sometimes a theme switch is enough, sometimes the CMS itself needs updating. Check: Is your site running PHP 8.1+? Is SSL active? Does it look usable on a phone? If any answer is no, start there.

cta.label: Send me the URL – I’ll check the foundation

result-redesign-content.title: Outdated content – often solved faster than expected.
result-redesign-content.text: Before commissioning a redesign: check if new text and photos are enough. Checklist: Are the service descriptions still accurate? Are all staff members current? Are the contact details correct? Are the photos older than 5 years? A content update costs a fraction of a redesign and often delivers more, because visitors read content, not admire layouts.

cta.label: Send me the URL – I’ll tell you what needs to change

result-redesign-komplett.title: Complete redesign – how to plan it sensibly.
result-redesign-komplett.text:
When design, technology and content are all outdated, a fresh start pays off. But not without a plan. Step 1: Define what the new website must do (not what it should have). Step 2: Check what works well on the old site (analytics, most visited pages). Step 3: Only then decide on design and technology. The most common mistake: commissioning a design before it's clear what the website should achieve.

Typical effort: 60–120 hours incl. concept and implementation.

cta.label: Let’s plan the fresh start

result-performance.title: The three most common bottlenecks – and what you can do yourself.
result-performance.text:
First: images. Uncompressed PNGs are the most common reason for slow sites. Use WebP, limit max width to 1920px. Second: too much JavaScript. Slider plugins, chat widgets, analytics scripts – each costs load time. Check what you really need. Third: bad hosting. Shared hosting at EUR 3/month is slow, period. Self-check: pagespeed.web.dev. Above 90 is good, below 50 is a problem.

Typical effort: 5–15 hours for analysis and optimisation.

cta.label: Send me the URL – initial assessment free

result-performance-wp.title: WordPress slow – the five usual suspects.
result-performance-wp.text:
1. Too many plugins. Above 20 gets critical, above 30 your site is guaranteed to be slow. Deactivate everything you don't use daily. 2. Page builders (Elementor, Divi). They generate bloated HTML code. 3. No caching plugin. WP Super Cache or W3 Total Cache are free and deliver 30-50% improvement immediately. 4. Images not compressed. ShortPixel or Imagify solve this automatically. 5. PHP version too old. Anything below PHP 8.1 is slower and insecure. Switchable in the hosting panel.

Typical effort: 5–15 hours. Often faster than expected.

cta.label: Send me the URL – initial assessment free

result-performance-andere.title: Not WordPress – still slow?
result-performance-andere.text:
The universal bottlenecks apply everywhere: large images, too much JavaScript, bad hosting. Check pagespeed.web.dev for what Google says. The most common hints: Largest Contentful Paint too high (large image above fold), Total Blocking Time too high (JavaScript blocking), Cumulative Layout Shift (elements jump while loading). Shopify shops often slow down due to apps – each app loads its own JavaScript. Deactivate apps you don't need.

Typical effort: 5–15 hours for analysis and optimisation.

cta.label: Send me the URL – initial assessment free

result-bfsg.title: European Accessibility Act – what it means for your website.
result-bfsg.text:
Mandatory since June 2025, fines up to EUR 100,000. The standard is WCAG 2.1 AA. The three most common errors: too little contrast (light text on light background), no keyboard navigation (Tab key must reach all links), missing alt texts on images. Self-check: install the WAVE browser extension – it shows errors directly on the page. Or test whether you can operate your own website using only the keyboard. If you get stuck somewhere, your users have the same problem.

Typical effort: 10–30 hours for audit and implementation.

cta.label: Let’s check your website

result-bfsg-fix.title: Fixing accessibility issues – what it costs and takes.
result-bfsg-fix.text:
Depends on the scope. The most common fixes: adjusting contrasts (1-2 hours), adding alt texts (1 hour per 20 images), fixing keyboard navigation (2-8 hours depending on theme), making forms accessible (2-4 hours). For a typical WordPress site with 10-20 pages: 8 to 20 hours of effort. I document the changes so you can prove what was done in case of an audit. Initial assessment free.

Typical effort: 5–20 hours, depending on the number of errors.

cta.label: Send your errors – I’ll estimate the effort

result-seo.title: SEO vs. GEO – what has changed.
result-seo.text: Classic SEO still works. But: 73% of B2B buyers now research with ChatGPT, and 61% fewer clicks land on classic Google results since AI Overviews. Your website must be readable not just for Google but for every AI system. Quick test: Ask ChatGPT about your company. If the answer is thin or wrong, you have a GEO problem. What you can do yourself: write clear, factual text. Concrete statements instead of marketing phrases.

cta.label: Send me the URL – I’ll check Google and AI

result-seo-google.title: Google SEO – what still works in 2026.
result-seo-google.text: The basics don't change: clean titles and descriptions, fast load times (Core Web Vitals), mobile optimisation, internal linking. What many forget: Google Search Console. Free, shows you exactly which terms you rank for and where you're losing clicks. Register at search.google.com/search-console. What has changed: AI Overviews push organic results down. Structured data (Schema.org) helps remain visible in the remaining results.

cta.label: Send me the URL – I’ll set up the tools

result-seo-ki.title: AI visibility – how to check it yourself.
result-seo-ki.text: Open ChatGPT and ask about your company. Is the answer correct? Complete? If ChatGPT doesn't know you or says incorrect things, you have a problem that no classic SEO solves. What helps: clear, factual text on your website. Concrete statements instead of marketing phrases. Structured data (Schema.org, JSON-LD) so AI systems can categorise your content. Machine-readable endpoints. And a robots.txt that doesn't block AI crawlers. I set up the technical side in every project.

cta.label: Try the ChatGPT test – then let’s talk

result-technik.title: First aid before you call someone.
result-technik.text: Three things you can check yourself: First: try a different browser. If the problem only occurs in one browser, it's the cache – Ctrl+Shift+Del, clear cache, reload. Second: deactivate plugins. Open WordPress admin, deactivate all plugins, check if the problem is gone. Then reactivate one by one. Third: open the browser console (F12) and look for red error messages. If it says 403 or 500, it's a server problem.

cta.label: Describe the problem – initial assessment free

result-technik-wp.title: WordPress problems – solve systematically.
result-technik-wp.text: Step 1: WordPress Health Check (under Tools, Site Health). Shows known issues. Step 2: Deactivate all plugins, switch to default theme (Twenty Twenty-Four). Problem gone? Then it's a plugin or theme issue. Reactivate plugins one by one until the error returns. Step 3: Check PHP version (hosting panel). Anything below 8.1 is outdated. Step 4: If nothing helps – Recovery Mode. WordPress sends a link by email when the site crashes. You can still log in with it.

cta.label: Describe the problem – initial assessment free

result-technik-woo.title: WooCommerce problems – a different league.
result-technik-woo.text: WooCommerce bugs often involve money: orders don't go through, prices are wrong, payment provider reports errors. Don't experiment on your own while the shop is live. What you can check: Are all plugins up to date? Is a caching plugin caching the cart (common error)? Are shipping settings correct? Does the test order work in the payment provider's sandbox mode? For payment issues: always check with the payment provider (Stripe, PayPal, Mollie) first for logged errors.

cta.label: Describe the problem – shops get priority

result-betreuung-wp.title: WordPress maintenance – what's really needed.
result-betreuung-wp.text:
WordPress updates without oversight can break your site. A plugin update breaks another, a theme update changes the layout. That's why I update manually, check afterwards and roll back if needed. Basic (from EUR 15/month): updates, backups, security monitoring. You notice nothing, and that's exactly the point. Standard (from EUR 80/month): plus small changes, new content. You send an email, I implement it. What you actually need depends on how often your site changes.

Basic from EUR 15/month. Standard from EUR 80/month.

cta.label: Let’s talk about your website

result-betreuung-woo.title: WooCommerce maintenance – more effort than WordPress alone.
result-betreuung-woo.text:
A shop needs more attention than a company website. Payment providers change their APIs, WooCommerce updates can break the checkout, tax rules change. Plus: order monitoring, plugin compatibility, performance monitoring as the catalogue grows. I recommend at least Standard maintenance (from EUR 80/month) for active shops. Basic only works if the shop barely has orders. For business-critical shops: Premium, so problems are resolved the same day.

Basic from EUR 40/month. Standard from EUR 80/month.

cta.label: Let’s talk about your shop

result-betreuung-statisch.title: Static website – honestly: you barely need anything.
result-betreuung-statisch.text:
No CMS, no database, no plugins, no updates. Static websites are low-maintenance by definition. SSL certificate, domain renewal, done. If you host on GitHub Pages or Netlify, that costs EUR 0. What you might still need: someone to make code changes when content changes. And someone to keep an eye on the hosting. For that, Basic maintenance (from EUR 15/month) or simply hourly billing is enough.

Changes billed by effort. No ongoing contract needed.

cta.label: Get in touch when needed – no contract required

result-betreuung-premium.title: Premium – when the website is business-critical.
result-betreuung-premium.text:
Some companies don't need a maintenance contract but their own developer. Just without social contributions, holidays and office costs. Premium means: I'm your dedicated contact for development, design, strategy and compliance. Priority support, retainer, short paths. That's how I work with Gerwing Steinwerke – sole technical responsibility since 2023. This pays off when your website directly generates revenue or when downtime costs money.

Monthly retainer: EUR 400–800/month. Your own developer without fixed costs.

cta.label: Let’s discuss the retainer

result-geo.title: How to check your AI visibility yourself.
result-geo.text:
Quick test: Open ChatGPT and ask about your company. Is the answer correct and complete? If not, you have a problem. What you can do without a developer: write clear, factual text. No marketing phrases, but concrete statements. 'We manufacture concrete paving stones in Osnabrueck since 1968' – instead of: 'We are your partner for premium outdoor design.' What needs technical work: Schema.org, JSON-LD, machine-readable endpoints. This website here is the example – ask ChatGPT about me.

GEO optimisation is included in every project. Not because it's free, but because it's pointless to leave it out.

cta.label: Try the ChatGPT test – then let’s talk

result-geo-audit.title: Audit – what I specifically check.
result-geo-audit.text:
First: Does ChatGPT find your company, and is what it says correct? Second: Is structured data present (Schema.org, JSON-LD)? Third: Is your content machine-readable or hidden behind JavaScript? Fourth: Do you have a robots.txt that doesn't block AI crawlers? You get a concrete report with priorities – what should be fixed immediately and what can wait.

Audit effort: 5–10 hours. 15 minutes on the phone to get started.

cta.label: Request an audit – 15 minutes is all it takes

result-vergleich-kontakt.title: You asked the right questions.
result-vergleich-kontakt.text: Most people only ask about price. You asked about the how. That speaks to good decisions. My answers to the five freelancer questions: 1. Live websites: 20+ of them, see the references below. 2. Handover: everything documented, standard technologies, no proprietary code. 3. Accessibility: included in every project. 4. Response time: 24 hours, same-day for Premium. 5. Post-project work: EUR 80/hour, effort estimated upfront. If that works for you – let's talk.

cta.label: Ask me your five questions – I’ll answer honestly
